summary: Gruber tackles the widespread misconception that force quitting iOS apps improves battery life and performance. He explains that iOS freezes background apps, reclaiming their RAM and CPU, so unfreezing them uses far less energy than relaunching force-quit apps. He cites Craig Federighi, Steve Jobs, Apple's official documentation, and multiple tech writers to support his case. He notes the irony that iOS's excellent app-freezing is so seamless it actually perpetuates the myth, since users assume apps never stopped running. He calls out Apple Store Genius Bar staff for spreading the bad advice and frames iOS's background management as a major technical advantage over Android that habitual force-quitters are negating.
key_points: iOS freezes background apps so effectively that unfreezing uses far less CPU and energy than relaunching a force-quit app
Force quitting apps actually worsens battery life and app-switching performance, the opposite of what most people believe
The seamless speed of unfreezing apps ironically reinforces the myth, since users assume apps were running the whole time
YouTube speed test benchmarks demonstrate iOS's advantage: iPhones reload apps from background nearly instantly while Android phones must relaunch them
Even Apple Store Genius Bar staff have been caught spreading this misconception to customers
money_quotes: The single biggest misconception about iOS is that it's good digital hygiene to force quit apps that you aren't using.
Not only does force quitting your apps not help, it actually hurts.
Like with any voodoo, there are die-hard believers.
An awful lot of very hard work went into making iOS work like this. It's a huge technical advantage that iOS holds over Android.

Every iPhone user in the world who habitually force quits background apps manually is wasting all of the effort that went into this while simultaneously wasting their own device's battery life and making everything slower for themselves.
Apps frozen in the background on iOS unfreeze so quickly that I think it actually helps perpetuate the myth that you should force quit them.
key_insight: iOS's background app management is so well-engineered that force quitting apps actively works against the system's design, wasting battery and degrading performance rather than improving them.
